verses.commentary: This is the written account of Adam's family line. The Hebrew word (toledot, תּוֹלְדוֹת) means generations or genealogy. This structures Genesis into sections. When God created mankind, he made them in the likeness of God. The image of God persists after the fall. Humanity retains dignity despite sin.
He created them male and female and blessed them. Echoes Genesis 1:27-28. When they were created, he called them mankind (adam, אָדָם). Both male and female together constitute humanity.

When Adam had lived 130 years, he had a son in his own likeness, in his own image and named him Seth. Just as Adam was made in God's image, Seth is made in Adam's image. The image passes down but now includes fallenness. The genealogy begins.

After Seth was born, Adam lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters. Long lifespans characterize the pre-flood world. The numbers may be symbolic or reflect different conditions. The pattern is: age at son's birth, years lived after, other children, total age, then he died.

Altogether Adam lived a total of 930 years and then he died. The refrain and then he died echoes throughout the chapter like a drumbeat. Death is universal. The warning of Genesis 2:17 proves true. Mortality reigns.
When Seth had lived 105 years, he became the father of Enosh. The genealogy continues through Seth, the line of hope (4:25-26), not through Cain.
After he became the father of Enosh, Seth lived 807 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern repeats. Life goes on. Generations multiply.
Altogether Seth lived a total of 912 years and then he died. The drumbeat continues. Death comes for all.
When Enosh had lived 90 years, he became the father of Kenan. Enosh means mortal or mankind, emphasizing human frailty.
After he became the father of Kenan, Enosh lived 815 years and had other sons and daughters. The generations continue steadily.
Altogether Enosh lived a total of 905 years and then he died. The refrain again. No one escapes death.
When Kenan had lived 70 years, he became the father of Mahalalel. The genealogy marches forward through time.
After he became the father of Mahalalel, Kenan lived 840 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern is consistent, emphasizing continuity.
Altogether Kenan lived a total of 910 years and then he died. Death remains the refrain.
When Mahalalel had lived 65 years, he became the father of Jared. Mahalalel may mean praise of God, a hopeful name.
After he became the father of Jared, Mahalalel lived 830 years and had other sons and daughters. The generations continue.
Altogether Mahalalel lived a total of 895 years and then he died. The drumbeat of mortality continues.
When Jared had lived 162 years, he became the father of Enoch. We are approaching a significant figure.
After he became the father of Enoch, Jared lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern holds.
Altogether Jared lived a total of 962 years and then he died. The longest lifespan so far, yet death still comes.
When Enoch had lived 65 years, he became the father of Methuselah. Enoch's story will break the pattern.

After he became the father of Methuselah, Enoch walked faithfully with God 300 years and had other sons and daughters. Walked with God (hithalech, הִתְהַלֵּךְ) suggests intimate, ongoing relationship. Only Enoch and Noah receive this description in Genesis. Faithfulness is possible even in a fallen world.
Altogether Enoch lived a total of 365 years. His lifespan is dramatically shorter than others, but the significance lies not in length but in what follows.

Enoch walked faithfully with God; then he was no more, because God took him away. The refrain and then he died is broken! Enoch does not die but is taken by God. Death is not absolute. There is a way beyond mortality. Hope interrupts the drumbeat.
When Methuselah had lived 187 years, he became the father of Lamech. Methuselah will have the longest recorded lifespan in Scripture.
After he became the father of Lamech, Methuselah lived 782 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern resumes after Enoch's exception.
Altogether Methuselah lived a total of 969 years and then he died. The oldest person in the Bible. Yet death still comes. Methuselah's death likely coincides with the flood, a boundary marker.
When Lamech had lived 182 years, he had a son. This Lamech is distinct from Cain's descendant (4:18-24). He belongs to Seth's line.

He named him Noah (Noach, נֹחַ) and said, He will comfort us in the labor and painful toil of our hands caused by the ground the LORD has cursed. Noah's name means rest or comfort. Lamech expresses hope that Noah will bring relief from the curse of Genesis 3:17. This anticipates the flood and new beginning.
After Noah was born, Lamech lived 595 years and had other sons and daughters. The pattern continues.
Altogether Lamech lived a total of 777 years and then he died. The number seven suggests completeness or perfection. Death comes even to the father of Noah.

After Noah was 500 years old, he became the father of Shem, Ham, and Japheth. The genealogy reaches Noah, the hinge figure. From him will come a new humanity after the flood. The stage is set for the next act of the drama.
